story: NOAA Fisheries' GAIA (Geospatial Artificial Intelligence for Animals), with USGS, is building an experimental machine-learning system to detect endangered Cook Inlet beluga whales in very-high-resolution satellite imagery.

fact_check_safe_set.label: best current Cook Inlet abundance estimate, 2022 aerial survey (range 290-386). NOT an AI result.
fact_check_safe_set.source: noaa beluga species page + feature story

fact_check_safe_set.fact: may be stabilizing and possibly increasing, ~0.2%/yr 2012-2022
fact_check_safe_set.label: NOAA, hedged ('possibly')

fact_check_safe_set.fact: GAIA integrates very-high-resolution satellite imagery with cloud computing, geospatial analysis, and machine learning
fact_check_safe_set.label: machine learning (NOAA's word) - experimental, building an automated detection system, NOT deployed

fact_check_safe_set.fact: training datasets built with expert human annotation
fact_check_safe_set.label: human-in-the-loop; not a finished autonomous detector

fact_check_safe_set.fact: Partners: NOAA Fisheries (lead), USGS, Microsoft AI for Good, Naval Research Laboratory
fact_check_safe_set.label: verbatim NOAA partner list (also Maxar, ESRI, NGA, NRO)

fact_check_safe_set.fact: automates satellite imagery retrieval via the USGS EarthExplorer API
fact_check_safe_set.label: verbatim NOAA

fact_check_safe_set.fact: June 2025: tasking aligned with the aerial abundance survey yielded no imagery that month, likely due to competing priorities near the Port of Anchorage and Joint Base Elmendorf-Richardson
fact_check_safe_set.label: NOAA example; cause stated as 'likely', not fact

fact_check_safe_set.fact: source pages last updated June 2026 (satellite-monitoring 06/25/2026, GAIA 06/18/2026)
fact_check_safe_set.label: freshness

attributable_quote.text: While this is certainly encouraging news, it is important to recognize that abundance estimates can vary from year to year due to a number of factors.
attributable_quote.speaker: Kim Goetz, marine mammal biologist, Alaska Fisheries Science Center
attributable_quote.note: about the abundance estimate, not GAIA

honesty_guardrails: Say 'machine learning' (NOAA's word), never 'deep learning' or a named architecture.
Never attribute the 331 count to GAIA/satellite - it is an aerial-survey number.
The AI system is EXPERIMENTAL: building training datasets, not a deployed counter. Do not imply it operationally counts belugas.
The military-airspace cause is NOAA's 'likely', not confirmed fact.
No accuracy/detection-rate figures exist for belugas - do not invent any.
Belugas in turbid glacial-silt water are genuinely hard to see from above - a hard problem, not a solved one.
counterpoints: The detector is early-stage/experimental, not replacing aerial surveys.
The pipeline can be starved of imagery (June 2025 yielded none).
The tool itself does not save whales; the population trend is driven by other factors.
The hopeful trend (possibly increasing) is a survey result, hedged by NOAA.
